data.title: Trump’s new 10% global tariff to take effect on Feb. 24
data.description:
President Donald Trump’s newly introduced 10% global tariff will be in force for 150 days after taking effect on Feb. 24, the White House announced late Friday after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down most of the tariffs he imposed last year.

The announcement came after Trump signed a proclamation, invoking authority under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 to impose the new import duty. The legislation allows the president “to address certain fundamental international payment problems through surcharges and other special import restrictions," the White House said.

“By taking this action, the United States can stem the outflow of its dollars to foreign producers and incentivize the return of domestic production.”

Earlier in the day, the Supreme Court issued a 6-3 ruling arguing that Trump exceeded his powers when he invoked the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 to set sweeping tariffs against more than 100 countries last year.

The landmark judgment drew a sharp rebuke from the president, prompting him to impose the new temporary import duty, which, according to the White House, is now expected to take effect on Feb. 24 at 12:01 a.m. ET and last for 150 days.

Dear readers, we recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Vanda surges as FDA approves Bysanti for bipolar disorder, schizophrenia
data.description:
• The U.S. FDA has approved Vanda Pharmaceuticals' (VNDA) Bysanti (milsaperidone) for the treatment of bipolar I disorder and schizophrenia.
  • The company's shares are up ~44% in after-hours trading Friday.
  • Milsaperidone is an active metabolite of iloperidone, which was approved in 2009 and sold by Vanda under the brand name Fanapt.
  • Studies of milsaperidone found it bioequivalent to iloperidone.
  • Bysanti is also in phase 3 as an adjunctive treatment for major depressive disorder.

data.title: Supreme Court tariff ruling seen curbing Trump's ability to use energy as a weapon
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court's rejection of President Trump's tariffs imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act will limit his power to use energy to pressure trading partners, analysts told Axios on Friday, after having aggressively used tariffs - even just the threat of tariffs - to gain leverage in reaching trade agreements.

Many of Trump's deals have included pledges to buy U.S. oil and liquefied natural gas, and the president has frequently threatened tariffs to pressure countries not to buy Russian or Iranian oil, but the Supreme Court ruling will "absolutely will limit Trump's ability to wield tariffs for geopolitical pressure," Trevor Sutton, a trade researcher with Columbia University's Center on Global Energy Policy, told Axios.

"One of the oddest and most innovative aspects of Trump's use of IEEPA was the way he used them as quasi-sanctions to punish countries for trade that did not involve the United States," Sutton said, citing India's purchase of Russian oil as an example.

The tariff ruling has little direct effect on crude oil and natural gas, but for energy-related companies, steel tariffs that remain under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act will continue to impact domestic drilling costs, and duties on solar equipment from several Asian countries remain under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974.

Oil States International (OIS) CEO Cindy Taylor said the court ruling is "welcome news" that could provide a more predictable cost structure for companies that manufacture equipment and provide field services for the oil and gas industry, helping them regain some of their competitive edge; Oil States shares soared 25% on Friday.

The tariff rate on certain Oil States (OIS) goods increased from 25% to 98% in mid-2025, Taylor said on the company's earnings conference call, and "with the weaker market in the U.S. and a lot of competition that came into the market, it was very hard to pass those costs on to our customers."

Oil futures ended little changed Friday while posting strong weekly gains on increased concerns about potential U.S. military action against Iran.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for March delivery fell $0.04 on Friday to $66.39/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for April delivery ended +0.1% at $71.76/bbl; for the week, the Nymex and Brent benchmarks gained 5.6% and 5.9%, respectively.

U.S. natural gas (NG1:COM) snapped a three-session losing streak on Friday with a cold shot across the eastern U.S. expected to raise demand in the next few days; the Nymex front-month March contract closed +1.7% to $3.047/MMBtu; for the week, the front-month rose 6%.

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), finished the week +0.9%.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Berkshire's PacifiCorp to pay $575M settlement of U.S. government's wildfire claims
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.A) (BRK.B) PacifiCorp utility agreed to pay $575M to resolve U.S. government claims for damages resulting from six wildfires in Oregon and California that burned nearly 290K acres of federal land, the U.S. Department of Justice said Friday.

The settlement resolves the government's claims that PacifiCorp's electrical lines negligently started all six fires and will help repay the substantial costs incurred in fighting the fires, the DoJ said.

Five of the fires - Archie Creek, Echo Mountain Complex, Slater, South Obenchain, and 242 - began in September 2020 and burned ~250K acres of federal land, and the sixth fire, McKinney, began in July 2022 and burned 39K acres of federal land.

The claims resolved by the settlement are allegations only, and there has been no determination of liability; the company denied liability in agreeing to the settlement.

Earlier this week, PacifiCorp said it agreed to sell many of its assets in Washington state to Portland General Electric for $1.9B, citing the need to boost liquidity as it defends against wildfire litigation.

data.title: AMH outlines 2026 core FFO growth target of up to $1.95 per share while moderating development pace
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bryan Smith emphasized AMH’s strategic positioning as a provider of single-family rentals amid ongoing national discussions about housing affordability, stating, “We appreciate the attention to this critical issue and continue to emphasize that AMH is part of the solution.” He highlighted that over 5,000 households, accounting for approximately 30% of all move-outs in 2025, left AMH homes to purchase a house. Smith noted the company’s focus on ground-up development, with over 14,000 newly built homes added since 2017, and reported that in 2025, AMH sold over 1,800 homes to individual homeowners, with a similar volume expected in 2026. The CEO announced plans to deliver around 1,900 newly constructed homes in 2026, maintaining the company’s strategy of funding development through disposition proceeds.

  • Smith reported, “In 2025, we delivered $1.87 of core FFO per share, representing year-over-year growth of 5.4%.” He described a challenging environment at the close of 2025, with “seasonal demand moderation and stubborn supply” pressuring rates and occupancy entering 2026.

  • CFO Christopher Lau stated, “We closed out 2025 with solid execution, generating quarterly net income attributable to common shareholders of $123.8 million or $0.33 per diluted share and $0.47 of quarterly core FFO per share in unit, representing 4.1% year-over-year growth.” Lau highlighted full-year net income of $439 million or $1.18 per diluted share. He detailed the delivery of 2,300 homes through the development program and the sale of 1,827 properties for $570 million in net proceeds at an average disposition cap rate in the high 3% range.

  • Lau disclosed that AMH fully utilized its $265 million share repurchase authorization, buying back 8.4 million common shares at $31.65 per share. He also announced a new $500 million share repurchase authorization approved by the Board.

Outlook

  • Lau provided initial 2026 guidance, stating, “For the full year, we expect core FFO per share unit of $1.89 to $1.95, which at the midpoint represents year-over-year growth of 2.7%.” He outlined expectations for same-home core revenues growth of 2.25%, average monthly realized rent growth in the 2.5% range, and a 25 basis point year-over-year occupancy headwind.

  • The outlook includes property operating expense growth of 2.75%, with property tax growth in the 3% area and mid 2% growth on other expenses. Same-home core NOI growth is expected at 2% at the midpoint.

  • AMH plans to deploy around $750 million in total capital, including joint ventures, to add approximately 1,900 newly constructed homes. The wholly-owned portfolio is set to see $550 million of capital investment, with 1,400 homes funded entirely by recycled disposition capital.

Financial Results

  • AMH reported $123.8 million in net income attributable to common shareholders for the quarter and $439 million for the full year. Core FFO per share for Q4 was $0.47, with full-year core FFO per share at $1.87. The company delivered 490 homes in Q4 and sold 646 properties for roughly $190 million in net proceeds during the quarter.

  • At year-end, net debt to adjusted EBITDA was 5.2x, the revolving credit facility had a $360 million balance, and $110 million in cash was available. The company completed $115 million in share repurchases in January 2026.

Q&A

  • Eric Wolfe, Citigroup Inc.: Asked about expectations for occupancy and rent growth curves; Lincoln Palmer explained, “We’re highly focused on kind of building occupancy throughout leasing season, supported by some price action and then expect a flatter kind of peak of that occupancy and then holding more into the back of the year.” Lau added, “On the full year context, we’re thinking about new leases in about the flattish area for full year '26 renewals kind of consistently in the plus or minus 3% area.”

  • James Feldman, Wells Fargo: Inquired about variability in guidance. Smith noted that “supply across all aspects of residential, different housing types seems to be stubbornly elevated,” but demand for AMH products remains strong.

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Asked about development yields and capital deployment. Smith reported yields in the 5.3% area, similar to 2025, and Lau discussed balancing development with buybacks, highlighting a “patient approach to the timing of any additional repurchases.”

  • Haendel St. Juste, Mizuho Securities: Requested details on OpEx and turnover. Lau described 2025 as “one of our lowest property tax growth years in company history” and expects 3% property tax growth in 2026 along with a double-digit decrease in insurance costs.

  • Jeff Spector, BofA Securities: Sought specificity on supply pressures by market. Palmer detailed impacts in San Antonio, Phoenix, and Las Vegas, with continued strength in the Midwest.

  • David Segall, Green Street: Asked about funding for additional buybacks. Lau confirmed “a couple of hundred million dollars of incremental capital capacity already on the balance sheet in the form of leverage capacity” and noted the runway for dispositions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly cautious tone, frequently probing about occupancy trends, supply pressures, and the effect of political developments. Their questions reflected some concern over guidance variability and the impact of new regulatory attention.

  • Management’s tone during prepared remarks was confident, with Smith stating, “AMH... is committed to doing its part,” but shifted to a more measured and cautious approach in Q&A, frequently referencing market uncertainty and a “patient approach” to capital deployment.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment was more probing on regulatory and supply risk, while management showed increased caution in discussing market headwinds and development pacing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for core FFO per share in 2026 ($1.89 to $1.95) represents lower year-over-year growth (2.7% at midpoint) compared to the previous year’s 5.4% growth.

  • Management’s language shifted from highlighting industry-leading growth to a focus on moderating development activity and prudent capital allocation due to capital market and regulatory uncertainties.

  • Analysts’ questions moved from operating efficiencies and regional performance to a sharper focus on occupancy risk, supply pressures, and potential regulatory actions.

  • Management’s confidence in prepared remarks remained steady, but Q&A responses demonstrated a more cautious outlook, reflecting the evolving environment and a need for flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified elevated supply across several markets, particularly in multifamily and build-to-rent sectors, as a persistent challenge. Smith noted, “Supply across all aspects of residential... seems to be stubbornly elevated.”

  • Regulatory risk was highlighted, especially regarding potential policy changes affecting institutional single-family rental ownership. Smith stated, “There’s a lot still moving in the definitions and just on how this is all going to ultimately shake out.”

  • Analysts raised concerns about occupancy trends, development yields, and funding for buybacks amidst market uncertainty.

  • Lau emphasized the importance of maintaining balance between development activity, capital structure, and buybacks, with disposition constraints noted due to the need for tenant move-outs.

Final Takeaway

AMH is entering 2026 with moderated growth expectations, focusing on delivering 1,900 new homes and targeting core FFO per share of $1.89 to $1.95. The company is navigating persistent supply pressures and potential regulatory changes, maintaining a cautious approach to development investment and share repurchases. Management remains committed to disciplined capital allocation, operational excellence, and leveraging disposition proceeds to fund new construction while closely monitoring market conditions and regulatory developments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Search Minerals settles $395K debt through shares-for-debt deals
data.description:
• Search Minerals (SMY:CA) entered into settlement agreements to resolve $395,496 in outstanding debt with certain creditors through shares-for-debt transactions.
  • The company will issue 790,992 common shares at a deemed price of $0.50/share to satisfy the debt.
  • The transactions are intended to preserve cash and strengthen the company’s balance sheet.

data.title: Gold and silver settle higher, with uncertainty over next steps following Trump tariff ruling
data.description:
Gold and silver futures closed higher Friday after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down most of President Trump's tariffs, which was followed by the president's announcement of a 10% global tariff for 150 days to replace some of his emergency duties.

Analysts said the uncertainty around next steps the Trump administration could take will support precious metals prices.

"Given that the administration will pursue additional tariffs by other, seemingly more limited and less sweeping, authorizations... the chapter of trade and tariff-related uncertainty likely is not over, even if it is less sweeping and less immediately volatile," RBC Capital's Christopher Louney said in a note.

"In the immediate aftermath of the headlines, the tariff ruling news, coupled with headlines that Russia sold 300K oz of gold, saw prices pare back, but only briefly, and price action thereafter was basically a wash in our view, supporting the idea that this was an expected outcome," according to Louney.

"At this point, gold markets are already in the 'what's next' stage, and we would not write off further trade and tariff uncertainty, even if it is likely set to take different but potentially more diminished forms," the analyst wrote.

Gold and silver were already showing strong gains early Friday following weaker‑than‑expected data on U.S. economic growth, with GDP slowing sharply to a 1.4% annualized rate in Q4, well below economists' forecast of 3%, as the government shutdown and softer consumer spending slowed activity.

Meanwhile, the U.S. Personal Consumption Expenditure index - a key inflation metric watched closely by the Federal Reserve - rose 0.4% in December, above expectations for a 0.3% increase.

The data shows "inflation is still present in the marketplace... but with GDP coming in lower, it suggests the economy is not close to a turning point," RJO Futures market strategist Bob Haberkorn said in a note. "There are still many unknowns and uncertainties around the U.S. economy, and that is supportive for gold."

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for February delivery closed +1.74% to $5,059.30/oz, and front-month Comex February silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +6.1% to $82.283/oz; for the week, gold edged up 0.7% and silver surged 5.7%.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Trinity Biotech falls below Nasdaq public float requirement
data.description:
• Trinity Biotech (TRIB) received notice on February 19, 2026, that it no longer complies with Nasdaq’s minimum market value of publicly held shares requirement.
  • The deficiency relates to Nasdaq rule, which mandates a minimum $15M public float for continued listing.

data.title: Infinico Metals shareholders approve 15-for-1 share consolidation
data.description:
• Infinico Metals (INFM:CA) shareholders approved a 15-for-1 consolidation of the company’s outstanding common shares.
  • The move is intended to facilitate future financing and business development activities.
  • The consolidation is expected to become effective on February 25, 2026, with a record date of February 24, 2026.

data.title: ConocoPhillips weighs selling Permian assets worth $2B - Bloomberg
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) is exploring a sale of ~$2B worth of its Permian Basin assets as part of a broader streamlining of its portfolio, Bloomberg reported Friday.

The assets, picked up over the years through deals with Concho Resources and Shell, reportedly are located in the Delaware Basin portion of the Permian.

ConocoPhillips (COP) is working with advisers to seek a buyer, with interest expected from strategic as well as private equity suitors, although deliberations are at an early stage, the report said.

The company said in its Q4 earnings release that it closed $3.2B in asset sales in 2025 and remains on track to meet its $5B sale target by the end of 2026 as it streamlines its business.
